title: Wedding Benches
summary: A stud-length 2x4-and-2x6 bench built for event seating — minimal cutting, angled legs, and a tie-in board that keeps it from wobbling when built in a batch.
description:
Designed around stud-length lumber specifically so a venue or planner building several of these at once can buy studs (already cut to a common length) instead of full boards, minimizing both waste and cutting time per bench. That's the whole reason for the design: fast, repeatable, and cheap enough that building ten of them for a wedding doesn't feel like ten separate furniture projects.

Angling a few of the leg screws rather than driving them all straight in is a small trick worth remembering — it resists the leg racking sideways under load in a way that a straight-in screw alone doesn't, since the angled screw is working somewhat in tension against that specific direction of movement. The tie-in board between the legs at the bottom is what actually keeps a bench like this from wobbling once people are sitting and standing on it all day; don't treat it as optional even though the bench will stand without it.

materials.name: Pine, 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: About 12 ft of 2x4 total needed for the legs and bracing, with some left over.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 45° off square, ends parallel — long point to short point measurement.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two seat top boards at 92-5/8", two legs at 14-1/2", four leg braces at 16-1/2", two leg cross braces at 8-1/2" and two tie-in boards at 17" from the Pine. Cut two seat support boards at 92-5/8" from the Pine. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to four 8 ft Pine and two 8 ft Pine. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Join the top boards
steps.body: Screw the 2x4 top boards to the 2x6 support boards, spacing screws every 8"–12" along the length.

steps.title: Attach the end boards
steps.body: Screw the end boards to both ends of the 2x4-and-2x6 assembly.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Screw the legs to the inside of the outer frame. Angle a few screws in on each leg rather than driving them all straight — this resists the leg racking sideways under load.

steps.title: Add the tie-in board
steps.body: Add a tie-in board between the two legs near the bottom, leaving about a 3" gap below it. This is what keeps the bench from wobbling once it's loaded with people sitting and standing — don't skip it.

images.alt: Simple stud-length wedding and event seating bench
